problem_summary: 2011 MCM Problem A (continuous): determine the shape of a snowboard half-pipe cross-section that maximizes a skilled snowboarder's "vertical air" (max height above the coping edge), then tailor the shape for other goals such as maximum twist/total rotation, and finally discuss the trade-offs needed to build a "practical" course. The paper recasts an open-ended "best shape" question into a constrained functional-optimization problem: choose the cross-section curve y(x) and the rider's path z(x) to maximize vertical air H_f and total degree of rotation Θ, subject to physical realism (energy conservation, friction, air drag) and competition constraints (5–8 tricks per run, safe radius of curvature).
models_used: Energy-conservation / energy-budget model of one 'cycle' (slide + fly): tracks E0, E_lost, W_human, W_G, E_reach with a conservation identity E0 = E_lost + mg·z_t·sinθ + W_G + W_human + E_reach
Newtonian force analysis on a curved surface: normal force N from centripetal term plus gravity projection, friction f = αN (Coulomb, proportional to normal force) + βv² (quadratic air drag)
First-order linear ODE for energy loss E_lost(x) derived by differentiating a variable-upper-limit integral ∫f·ds; solved in closed form via the integrating-factor formula E_lost = e^{∫a}·∫b·e^{−∫a}
Differential-geometry of the curve: radius of curvature ρ = (1+y'²)^{3/2}/y'' used both for normal force and angular velocity; arc length ds = √(1+y'²+z'²)dx
Projectile-motion model of the airborne 'fly': vertical air with no drag H_f = ẏ_t²/(2g cos²θ); closed-form with quadratic drag H_f = (m/2β)·ln(1 + βẏ_t²/(mg cosθ)); fly time and fly distance S_f

Rigid-body angular-momentum model for rotation: rider modeled as a uniform stick, L_z0 = (1/3)l²ω0 m with ω0 = √(ẋ_t²+ẏ_t²)/ρ0; total rotation Θ = ω·t_f with conservation of angular momentum in flight and moment-of-inertia change to spin up

Calculus of variations framing: E_lost and vertical air are functionals of the unknown y(x) and z(x); the optimal-shape problem is a functional-extremum problem, solved numerically rather than via Euler–Lagrange

Genetic Algorithm for global shape optimization: genes = control-point positions of B-spline / 3rd-order spline convex curves; roulette-wheel selection, uniform crossover, convexity-preserving random mutation; fitness = vertical air (or angular momentum, or hybrid score)
Constrained random generation of convex C² curves via cumulative-sum of random increments on the second-derivative/slope sequence k(i), normalized to hit fixed endpoint height H
Empirical hybrid scoring model (borrowed from Harding et al.): Predicted Score = 11.424·AAT + 0.013·ADR − 2.223, used as the GA objective to resolve the air-vs-rotation trade-off
key_techniques: Decomposing the motion into a repeatable 'cycle' (slide-up then airborne fly) and making mechanical-energy loss E_lost the single linking quantity between phases
Separating the design space into y(x) = pipe shape and z(x) = rider's chosen path, so 'the shape' and 'the skilled rider' are modeled as distinct, optimizable factors
Converting an integral equation with variable upper limit into a solvable first-order linear ODE by differentiation, then applying the integrating-factor closed form
Verification by cross-checking the analytical projectile solution against the numerical solver (error ratios 0–0.08%) to justify neglecting air drag during the fly
Anchoring all parameters (α≈0.03 snow friction, β≈0.15 drag, m≈60 kg from Shaun White, E0≈7000 J from drop-in ramp geometry) to cited references, not guesses
Validating the headline result against reality: computed ~7 m vertical air matches Shaun White and the known 12–20 ft range for men
One-factor-at-a-time local sensitivity sweeps (±10% on W, H, B, z_t) plus full grid enumeration for the global optimum, then GA for the free-form transition curve
Fitting GA-produced free-form optimal curves back to 2nd-order curves and recognizing them as ellipses, cross-validating against the FIS recommendation that the transition be elliptical
Reusing one objective-function machinery for three goals (vertical air, fly time, angular momentum) and then combining them via a literature-justified weighted score to handle the trade-off
why_it_won: "From a judge's view this paper wins on physical depth + rigorous closure + independent validation, the three things O-award continuous papers need. (1) It does not hand-wave the physics: it derives the full force balance on a curved 3D surface, models friction and quadratic drag correctly, and produces a genuine closed-form solution for the energy-loss ODE rather than just simulating. (2) It correctly identifies that 'best shape' is a calculus-of-variations problem over the functionals y(x) and z(x), states that honestly, and then makes a defensible engineering decision to solve it numerically with a Genetic Algorithm — showing mathematical maturity about what is and isn't analytically tractable. (3) It validates twice over: numerical-vs-analytical agreement to <0.1%, and the ~7 m vertical-air result matches real elite performance and the empirically-fit elliptical transition matches the FIS judges' manual. (4) It answers all three sub-questions, including the open-ended 'practical course' trade-offs (radius of curvature for snow adhesion, E_reach carryover across cycles, the 5–8 trick count limiting steepness), which is exactly the kind of real-world judgment judges reward. (5) The 'half-blood' hybrid-scoring narrative ties the whole paper together: it uses a published automated-scoring formula as the GA objective, so the final recommended shape optimizes an externally-credible competition metric rather than the team's own invented one."
section_highlights: "Memorable title ('A half-blood half-pipe, a perfect performance') framing the air-vs-rotation trade-off as a 'half-blood' compromise shape. Summary explicitly partitions the work: basic model (shape for max vertical air) + path adaptation (max rotation) + extended model (rider's subjective influence) + practical-feasibility discussion, then frankly states the limitation that no analytic optimal-shape solution is given. Strong background section grounds the problem in real geometry (FIS-recommended W=15m, H=3.5m, T=5m, B=5m) and cites sports-science work (Harding et al.) establishing air-time and rotation as the two judging criteria. Clean model-overview flowcharts (course shape → f → V → vertical air / path) before the math. The Numerical Computation section transparently derives every parameter from references. A dedicated Validity + Sensitivity pair: a table comparing analytical vs numerical (error 0–0.08%) and a ±10% sensitivity table. Per-variable sweep figures (vertical air vs width, height, flat, θ, z_t) each with a paired energy plot, then a Global Optimal block giving concrete recommended numbers (W=18m, H=3m, Flat=4m, z_t=6m, θ=20°). The GA pipeline is laid out as an explicit procedure (control points → random convex curves → gene = control-point positions → fitness = vertical air → roulette/crossover/mutation → optimal curve), and the optimized curves are repeatedly fitted back to ellipses to cross-check against literature. Explicit Strength and Weakness section with checkmarks. The recurring 'fit the optimum to a 2nd-order curve → it's an ellipse → matches FIS' move is a recurring structural payoff."
pitfalls_or_limitations: No analytic solution to the optimal-shape problem — the paper admits the functional extremum is too complex and falls back entirely on numerics/GA, so there is no proof of global optimality (GA gives a heuristic optimum only)

Rider modeled as a uniform stick / point mass with a single mechanical-energy budget; real snowboard edge mechanics, board flex, carving dynamics, and on-snow board behavior are explicitly excluded (listed as a weakness)

Friction model assumes the angle between board direction and velocity is constant so f=αN is velocity-independent — a strong simplification that ignores edging and steering losses the rider actually controls
Assumes the rider's body stays perpendicular to the tangential surface throughout, and a fixed crouch-then-stand-up energy injection W_human at the coping — idealized posture model

Several figures/data tables in the markdown are clearly OCR/extraction artifacts (e.g. 'y = x^(-z)' heatmap, runaway +9/+67 rows, E_reach values jumping to ~139000 J vs ~14000 J elsewhere) — unit/scale inconsistencies make some quantitative plots untrustworthy as transcribed
Air drag during the fly is neglected after a single check at one parameter set; not re-verified across the parameter sweeps where velocities differ
z(x)=k·τ(x) (path proportional to projected arc length) is asserted as energy-minimizing only 'in a simple constant-friction condition,' then used generally
Global optimum reported from coarse grid enumeration over a narrow box (W 13–18, H 2–6, etc.), so the 'global' claim is bounded by the chosen ranges
reusable_patterns: Cycle-decomposition + single linking quantity: split a periodic/repeating motion into phases and pick one conserved/transferred quantity (here mechanical energy E_lost / E_reach) to chain phases together — keeps a complex process tractable

Shape-vs-strategy factorization: separate the designed object (y(x), the pipe) from the agent's choices (z(x), the rider's path), optimize each, and present the rider's skill as its own modeled variable — directly answers 'optimize for a skilled user' prompts

Integral-equation → ODE → integrating-factor closed form: when energy/cost is a variable-upper-limit integral of state, differentiate to get a first-order linear ODE and solve in closed form for an interpretable result

Free-form curve optimization via GA over spline control points with constraint-preserving mutation (here convexity + C² + fixed endpoints) — a transferable recipe for any 'find the best shape/profile' continuous problem

Constrained random sample generation by building the second-difference/slope sequence from cumulative sums of random numbers and normalizing to endpoint constraints — a clean way to sample valid convex curves for a GA initial population

Double validation discipline: (a) analytical-vs-numerical agreement table with explicit error ratios, and (b) result-vs-real-world benchmark (Shaun White / FIS ellipse) — a template for the Validity section of any continuous paper
Reduce a free-form numerical optimum to a recognizable closed form (fit GA curve → ellipse) to gain interpretability and cross-check against domain literature

Multi-objective resolution via a literature-justified scoring function: when objectives conflict (air vs rotation), adopt an externally published metric (Predicted Score = 11.424·AAT + 0.013·ADR − 2.223) as the optimizer's objective instead of an arbitrary self-chosen weighting
Parameter-table-from-references pattern: dedicate a section to pinning every constant (friction, drag, mass, initial energy) to citations before any computation, so reviewers trust the numbers

Practical-trade-off closure: after the math, explicitly translate constraints into engineering limits (min radius of curvature for snow adhesion, E_reach carryover limiting how greedy one jump can be, trick-count capping steepness) to answer the open-ended 'practical' sub-question
